my_thoughts: Defense arrived on cue - they want "greatness" without the knowledge requirement. Next move: present a concrete harm case (doctor without knowledge harming patients) to force them to drop "power is greatest" or accept that ignorant power is dangerous. Expect either a clarifying question or a softened stance when the harm is vivid.
accumulated_insight: Two-turn pattern: T1: Student asserted value claim ("Yes - the greatest of human powers") - cooperative engagement via affirms value claim; they understand: rhetoric = greatest power (value claim), power = supremacy (unexamined use). The constraint_collapse mechanism tests: power without knowledge = can cause harm, forces choice between power and good use. T2: Student defended claim ("Not necessarily. But that doesn't lessen his power!") - defensive rationalization via pushes_back; they understand: power must remain "great" even if ignorant. Pattern: bold claim + consequence test + defense + [next: concrete misuse example]. They are grasping the consequence (harm risk) but clinging to "greatest" framing.
visible_response: Then you’ve admitted he acts without knowledge—and the man without knowledge harms himself the most.
